返回本批次

品味人工智能和法学硕士时代

Taste in the age of AI and LLMs

rajnandan.com 作者 speckx 分数 222 评论 184 2026/04/07 23:54
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 2026 年 4 月 3 日
  2. 人工智能和法学硕士很快改变了一件事：合格的产出现在很便宜。几分钟内即可生成登陆页面。产品备忘录可以出现在单个提示中。在任何人都努力决定公司真正相信什么之前，融资演讲稿可能看起来已经很完美了。这就是为什么品味已成为科技领域的一个严肃话题。当每个人都能生产出看起来不错的东西时，优势就会转移到判断力上。脱颖而出的人不再只是那些能够生产的人。他们能够分辨什么是通用的，什么是真实的，什么是值得进一步推动的。但还有第二点同样重要：味道并不是最终的答案。如果人类减少自己从人工智能输出中进行选择，他们就有可能成为机器主导流程的审查者，而不是对结果有真正利害关系的建设者。人工智能和法学硕士时代的真正机会不是成为更好的选择者。它将品味与环境、限制和意愿结合起来，打造出仅凭普通人无法实现的东西。味道实际上意味着什么
  3. 在这种情况下，品味与奢华、地位或个人审美品牌无关。这是关于不确定性下的区分。最有意义的工作并不附带完美的数据。你不会得到一个电子表格来告诉你哪个句子会引起客户关注，哪个功能值得一个月的工程时间，或者哪个设计从完美到容易被遗忘。你还得做出决定。味道体现在三个地方：
  4. 你注意到什么 你拒绝什么 你如何准确地解释感觉不对的地方
  5. 最后一部分比它最初出现的更重要。很多人可能会说，“这感觉不太好”。很少有人会说，“这失败了，因为它听起来和其他所有 SaaS 产品都一样”，或者“这种解释将监管限制融入到营销语言中，会让客户感到困惑。”当品味从氛围转向诊断时，它就变得有用。为什么人工智能和法学硕士会压平中间层

Original Summary

  1. Apr 3, 2026
  2. AI and LLMs have changed one thing very quickly: competent output is now cheap. A landing page can be generated in minutes. A product memo can appear in a single prompt. A pitch deck can look polished before anyone has done the hard work of deciding what the company actually believes. That is why taste has become a serious topic in tech. When everyone can produce something that looks decent, the advantage shifts to judgment. The people who stand out are no longer just the ones who can produce. They are the ones who can tell what is generic, what is true, and what is worth pushing further. But there is a second point that matters just as much: taste is not the final answer. If humans reduce themselves to selecting from AI outputs, they risk becoming reviewers of a machine-led process instead of builders with real stakes in the outcome. The real opportunity in the age of AI and LLMs is not to become a better selector. It is to combine taste with context, constraints, and the willingness to build something that could not have emerged from the average alone. What taste actually means
  3. In this context, taste is not about luxury, status, or personal aesthetic branding. It is about distinction under uncertainty. Most meaningful work does not come with perfect data. You do not get a spreadsheet that tells you which sentence will make a customer care, which feature is worth a month of engineering time, or which design crosses the line from polished to forgettable. You still have to decide. Taste shows up in three places:
  4. What you notice What you reject How precisely you can explain what feels wrong
  5. That last part matters more than it first appears. Many people can say, "this feels off." Far fewer can say, "this fails because it sounds like every other SaaS product," or "this explanation collapses a regulatory constraint into marketing language and will confuse the customer." Taste becomes useful when it moves from vibe to diagnosis. Why AI and LLMs flatten the middle

评论（默认中文）

danielvaughn 2026/04/08 00:30
不同意整体论点。人类的努力仍然是一条护城河。过去几个月我一直在创建一个几乎完全由人工智能生成的代码库。以这样的速度，我已经比其他方式走得更远了，但这仍然需要付出很大的努力，而且我仍然浪费时间去研究那些无法解决的功能。

判断力一如既往地重要，这是有道理的，尽管我不确定我是否会称之为品味。我发现你必须有一个极其清晰的产品愿景，以及用于描述该产品的极其清晰的语言，才能有效地使用人工智能。了解您的术语，了解您希望如何将功能划分为模块，了解您希望这些模块的接口是什么。

如果没有上述内容，你就会遇到开发人员在人工智能之前遇到的同样的问题——代码库变得一团乱七八糟，甚至人工智能也无法理清它，因为这种混乱已经嵌入到了它自己的上下文中。

Disagree with the overall argument. Human effort is still a moat. I've been spending the past couple of months creating a codebase that is almost entirely AI-generated. I've gotten way further than I would have otherwise at this pace, but it was still a lot of effort, and I still wasted time going down rabbit holes on features that didn't work out.

There's some truth in there that judgement is as important as ever, though I'm not sure I'd call it taste. I'm finding that you have to have an extremely clear product vision, along with an extremely clear language used to describe that product, for AI to be used effectively. Know your terms, know how you want your features to be split up into modules, know what you want the interfaces of those modules to be.

Without the above, you run into the same issue devs would run into before AI - the codebase becomes an incoherent mess, and even AI can't untangle it because the confusion gets embedded into its own context.

在 HN 中查看
taude 2026/04/08 00:36
我觉得你非常同意品味很重要：“我发现你必须有一个非常清晰的产品愿景......””

清晰的生产愿景表明您正在以正确的方式构建正确的东西 - 这需要大量的品味才能做到正确。 好的 PM 都有这个。优秀的工程师都具备这一点。 有远见的领导者有这个......

使用人工智能生成代码和其他工件的执行是一个技能问题。 但如果没有这样的品味，那就是你正在以一种革命性的方式构建正确的东西，具有正确的功能，并且使用起来会很愉快......

在过去的一个月里，我考察了三个非工程师氛围编码的企业，可以看出，如果没有品味，他们充其量只能打造出相当平庸的产品。 创始人还没有看到这一点。正如文章所说，他们只是让自己陷入平庸。 我认为任何真正优秀的产品经理都能够立即改进我看到的所有这些应用程序。

I feel like you're pretty strongly agreeing that taste is important: " I'm finding that you have to have an extremely clear product vision...""

Clear production vision that you're building the right thing in the right way -- this involves a lot of taste to get right. Good PMs have this. Good enginers have this. Visionary leaders have this....

The execution of using AI to generate the code and other artifacts, is a matter of skill. But without the taste that you're building the right thing, with the right features, in a revolutionary way that will be delightful to use....

I've looked at three non-engineer vibe-coded businesses in the past month, and can tell that without taste, they're building a pretty mediocre product at best. The founders don't see it yet. And like the article says, they're just setting themselves up for mediocrity. I think any really good PM would be able to improve all these apps I looked at almost immediately.

在 HN 中查看
arnorhs 2026/04/08 00:50
按照我的理解，原来的文章说_唯一_剩下的区别是品味，而你回复的评论是说“错了，还有其他东西，比如努力”。

我不一定将您回复的评论解释为“味道并不重要”，这似乎是您回复的内容，只是它不是唯一剩下的东西。

我同意品味能让你走得更远。我同意你提到的所有高品味的例子。

但即使品味无可挑剔，你仍然需要学习、尝试、有想法、改变主意等等……把所有这些都放在“品味”的桶里就是在延伸它。

然而，在付出努力时拥有良好的品味，比单独努力更能让你走得更远。事实上，光靠努力一事无成，光靠品味也一事无成。一旦你和这两个人结婚，你就会有所成就。

The way I understood it, the original article is saying the _only_ remaining differentiator is taste and the comment you replied to is saying "wrong, there are also other things, such as effort".

I don't necessarily interpret the comment you replied to as saying that "taste is not important", which seems like what you are replying to, just that it's not the only remaining thing.

I agree that taste gets you far. And I agree with all the examples of good taste that you brought up.

But even with impeccable taste, you still need to learn, try things, have ideas, change your mind etc.. putting all of that in the bucket of "taste" is stretching it..

However, having good taste when putting in the effort, gets your further than with effort alone. In fact, effort alone gets you nowhere, and taste alone gets you nowhere. Once you marry the two you get somewhere.

But you still need effort, its not only taste. "Only" means you can do it with no effort.

theowaway213456 2026/04/08 03:18
换句话说，需要付出巨大的努力才能将你的品味充分传达给人工智能。并不是每个人都愿意花时间或精力来做这件事！ （一旦我们有了更直接的大脑/计算机接口，这种努力就会下降，但我预计它不会完全消除）
In other words, it requires a tremendous amount of effort to fully communicate your tastes to the AI. Not everybody wants to expend the time or mental effort doing this! (Once we have more direct brain/computer interfaces, this effort will go down, but I expect it will not be eliminated fully)
在 HN 中查看
Shog9 2026/04/08 04:17
这是两天内我第二次在这里看到一个子线程，人们似乎在争论如果这些需求的目标是 LLM 系统，定义和沟通需求是否算作工作。

我很困惑为什么这甚至是一个问题。我们过去称之为“系统分析”，它就像......一个完整的屁股 职业。法学硕士似乎非常有能力使用输出，但他们甚至还没有接近第一个能够接受需求并将其转化为工作代码的软件系统（针对“需求”和“工作”的各种定义）。

我还怀疑直接的大脑接口是否会减少这项工作的工作量；我不认为“打字”或“英语”是这里的主要障碍，就像“起草”不是人们设计自己的汽车和房屋的主要障碍一样......任何傻瓜 认为 他们知道自己需要什么！

This is the second time in two days I've seen a subthread here with folks seemingly debating whether or not defining and communicating requirements counts as work if the target of those requirements is an LLM system.

I'm confused as to why this is even a question. We used to call this "systems analysis" and it was like... a whole-ass career. LLMs seem to be remarkably capable of using the output, but they're not even close to the first software systems sold as being able to take requirements and turn them into working code (for various definitions of "requirements" and "working").

I'm also skeptical that direct brain interfaces would make this any less work; I don't think "typing" or "english" are the major barriers here, anymore than "drafting" is the major barrier to folks designing their own cars and houses... Any fool thinks they know what they need!

ToucanLoucan 2026/04/08 04:57
作为一名软件工程师和一名创意人员，我绝对不希望为我生成 1,000 个版本的内容。我不在乎它是免费的还是便宜的。我想要 做东西。

我知道这个概念对于 HN 的许多用户群来说是非常陌生的，但我并没有进入编程或制作艺术来完成成品；这是一个必要的功能，当它实现时会很可爱，但最终，我从过程中获得乐趣。发现用户遇到的问题并解决它的过程。

是的，我确信克劳德可以比我更快地做到这一点（而且只需付出几英亩的雨林的代价！），但同样，你没有抓住重点。我 享受 工作。这对我来说不是缺点。

As both a software engineer and a creative, I absolutely do not want 1,000 versions of what I am trying to make generated for me. I don't care if it's free or even cheap. I want to make things.

I know this is a concept deeply alien to a lot of HN's userbase but I did not get into programming or making art to have finished products; that's a necessary function that is lovely when it's reached, but ultimately, I derive my enjoyment from The Process. The process of finding a problem a user has, and solving it.

And yes I'm sure Claude could do it faster than me (and only at the cost of a few acres of rainforest!) but again, you're missing the point. I enjoy the work. That is not a downside to me.

mrdonbrown 2026/04/08 06:56
这是一条被低估的评论。你可以拥有最好的产品，但人工智能不仅降低了竞争对手的工作量，而且还淹没了从对外销售到内容营销等传统的产品宣传方式。有时会让你怀疑是否还有顾客。
This is an underrated comment. You could have the best product out there, but AI has not only lowered the effort for competitors but has flooded traditional ways to get your product known, from outbound sales to content marketing. Sometimes make you question whether there are customers anymore.
在 HN 中查看
zer00eyz 2026/04/08 01:32
> 不同意整体论点。

它倾向于一个好的方向，但作者显然缺乏阐明实际问题或解决方案的语言和理解。他们根本不知道自己不知道什么。

> 人类的努力仍然是一条护城河。

也稍微偏离了目标。如果我坐下来，带着制作一条裤子的所有设备和用品，你们中的大多数人（绝大多数）都会生产出一条糟糕的裤子。

这并不是因为缺乏努力，而是因为缺乏技巧。

> 判断力一如既往地重要，

不重要，关键。它是技能和经验的产物。

可用性（这个词经常被使用）、成本、实用性，都是人们对产品的期望。可靠性是一项要求：引用社交网络“我们不会崩溃”。如果你想跟上步伐，那么可维护性。

> 在人工智能之前开发人员会遇到的问题 - 代码库变得一团乱

大泥球（https://www.laputan.org/mud/ ）已27岁，仍然适用。但所有代码库都有一种倾向（来自边缘情况），这些代码没有良好的行内解释，缺乏持久的工件。给我找一个旧的代码库，我敢打赌，我们可以找到一条引用系统中不再存在的错误号的注释。

作为一个行业，我们可能需要诚实地认识到，我们也需要成为更好的图书馆员和档案管理员。

话虽这么说，这篇文章应该得到赞扬，它至少试图开始进行我们应该进行和不应该进行的对话。

> Disagree with the overall argument.

It's leaning in a good direction, but the author clearly lacks the language and understanding to articulate the actual problem, or a solution. They simply dont know what they dont know.

> Human effort is still a moat.

Also slightly off the mark. If I sat one down with all the equipment and supplies to make a pair of pants, the majority of you (by a massive margin) are going to produce a terrible pair of pants.

Thats not due to lack of effort, rather lack of skill.

> judgement is as important as ever,

Not important, critical. And it is a product of skill and experience.

Usability (a word often unused), cost, utility, are all the things that people want in a product. Reliability is a requirement: to quote the social network "we dont crash". And if you want to keep pace, maintainability.

> issue devs would run into before AI - the codebase becomes an incoherent mess

The big ball of mud (https://www.laputan.org/mud/ ) is 27 years old, and still applies. But all code bases have a tendency to acquire cruft (from edge cases) that don't have good in line explanations, that lack durable artifacts. Find me an old code base and I bet you that we can find a comment referencing a bug number in a system that no longer exists.

We might as an industry need to be honest that we need to be better librarians and archivists as well.

That having been said, the article should get credit, it is at least trying to start to have the conversations that we should be having and are not.

stronglikedan 2026/04/08 01:19
> 以这种速度，我已经比其他方式走得更远了，但这仍然需要付出很大的努力，而且我仍然浪费时间去研究那些无法解决的功能。

当我了解完人工智能编写的代码的结构并检查其正确性和完整性时，这似乎与我自己编写的代码一样费力。我担心，在人工智能变得可信之前，这种情况将继续成为现实。

> I've gotten way further than I would have otherwise at this pace, but it was still a lot of effort, and I still wasted time going down rabbit holes on features that didn't work out.

By the time I'm done learning about the structure of the code that AI wrote, and reviewing it for correctness and completeness, it seems to be as much effort as if I had just written it myself. And I fear that will continue to be the reality until AIs can be trusted.

andai 2026/04/08 00:40
我认为你没有抓住要点。现在，努力是一条护城河，因为半人马（人类+人工智能）仍然击败人工智能，但这种差距每年都在缩小（并且表面上会缩小）。

他们的目标是复制人类劳动，他们正在缩小这一差距。一旦他们这样做了（也许几十年，但可能会发生），那么就只剩下“特殊的东西”了。品味、视觉……我们都将成为里克·鲁宾斯。

直到 2045 年，他们发布 RubinGPT

I think you're missing the point. Effort is a moat now because centaurs (human+AI) still beat AIs, but that gap gets smaller every year (and will ostensibly be closed).

The goal is to replicate human labor, and they're closing that gap. Once they do (maybe decades, but probably will happen), then only that "special something" will remain. Taste, vision... We shall all become Rick Rubins.

Until 2045, when they ship RubinGPT

pegasus 2026/04/08 01:19
> 但这个差距每年都在缩小（并且表面上会缩小）

只要你为人类构建软件（并且 全部 我们构建的软件最终是为人类服务的），您需要人类来掌舵，以引导这艘船走向人类友好的解决方案。

> but that gap gets smaller every year (and will ostensibly be closed)

As long as you build software for humans (and all software we build is for humans, ultimately), you'll need humans at the helm to steer the ship towards a human-friendly solution.

在 HN 中查看
boshalfoshal 2026/04/08 01:34
问题是，人类_需要_大多数软件吗？需要与人类交互的表面越少，就越不需要人类参与设计这些表面。

在一个假设的世界中，也许一些人工智能代理或助手会为你完成绝大多数随机任务，doordash 网站对你来说有多令人愉悦重要吗？如果有的话，它应该对人工智能代理来说看起来“不错”，以便更容易导航。也许“看起来不错”只是让一些公众 API 去做各种事情。

UI 是 API 的包装器。代理只需要使用API​​即可。

The thing is, do humans _need_ most software? The less surfaces that need to interact with humans, the less you need humans in the loop to design those surfaces.

In a hypothetical world where maybe some AI agents or assistants do the vast majority of random tasks for you, does it matter how pleasing the doordash website looks to you? If anything, it should look "good" to an ai agent so that its easier to navigate. And maybe "looking good" just amounts to exposing some public API to do various things.

UIs are wrappers around APIs. Agents only need to use APIs.

在 HN 中查看
pegasus 2026/04/08 04:07
> 人类_需要_大多数软件吗？

是的，如果不是多余的软件的话。最终的效用是对人类的。当然，在某些时候，人类停止编写汇编语言，而是使用编译器，因此抽象级别和接口发生了变化，但它仍然是为人类服务的。

拿你的例子来说，你认为人类会只想使用聊天界面与人工智能代理进行交互吗？对于当今人类使用计算机执行的大多数任务来说，这将非常笨重。因此UI将从网站迁移到AI代理界面。这一切都会发生变化，变得更加强大（希望如此！），但不会消失。编译器的出现如何导致世界上程序员的增加，人工智能代理也将如此。这也与贾文悖论有关。

> do humans _need_ most software?

Yes, if it's not redundant software. The ultimate utility is to a human. Sure, at some point humans stopped writing assembly language and employed a compiler instead, so the abstraction level and interfaces change, but it's all still there to serve humans.

To use your example, do you think humans will want to interact with AI agents using a chat interface only? For most tasks humans use computers today, that would be very unwieldy. So the UI will migrate from the website to the AI agent interface. It all transforms, becoming more powerful (hopefully!), but won't go away. And just how the advent of compilers led to an increase of programmers in the world, so will AI agents. This is connected with Javon's paradox as well.

在 HN 中查看
9rx 2026/04/08 02:10
> 也许“看起来不错”只是让一些公众 API 去做各种事情。

也许吧，但你仍然需要人类来做出这个决定。无论您添加多少间接内容，该软件仍然是为人类构建的。

可以想象有一天，这种情况不再成立，但当你达到这一点时，它就不再是人工智能了。

> And maybe "looking good" just amounts to exposing some public API to do various things.

Maybe, but you still need humans to make that call. The software is still built for humans no matter how much indirection you add.

There is a conceivable day where that is no longer true, but when you have reached that point it is no longer AI.

我认为与当前工作的差距可以在很大程度上缩小，但我们真的有信心这将适用于出现的新工作吗？我越来越认为我们对后人工智能世界中的工作缺乏想象力。 IE。算盘使用者能想象所有的计算机后工作吗？

monknomo 2026/04/08 01:13

do you need taste if you can massively parallel a/b test your way to something that is tasteful? say like you take your datacenter of geniuses and have a a rubin-loop supervising testing different directions. shouldn't that be close enough?
在 HN 中查看
all2 2026/04/08 02:17
这里的“味道”是一个棘手的解决方案。只要看看建筑在人类历史上是如何变化的，建筑材料、组装、形状、流程，所有这些都归结为品味。其中一些可以归结为“效率”——比如设计厨房的三点系统，但即使这也是一个品味问题。

找到三位专业厨师，他们会给你关于如何组织厨房的三种不同的愿景。

这同样适用于任何专业领域，包括软件工程。

"taste" here is an intractable solution. Just take a look at how architecture has varied throughout the history of mankind, building materials, assembly, shape, flow, all of it boils down to taste. Some of it can be reduced to 'efficiency' -- like the 3 point system for designing kitchens, but even that is a matter of taste.

Find three professional chefs and they will give you three distinct visions for how a kitchen should be organized.

The same goes for any professional field, including software engineering.

在 HN 中查看
rvz 2026/04/08 00:47
> 如果没有上述内容，你就会遇到开发人员在人工智能之前遇到的同样的问题——代码库变得一团乱七八糟，甚至人工智能也无法理清它，因为这种混乱已经嵌入到了它自己的上下文中。

我们对此有一个术语，称为“理解债”[0][1]。

[0] https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08942

[1] https://medium.com/@addyosmani/comprehension-debt-the-hidden...

> Without the above, you run into the same issue devs would run into before AI - the codebase becomes an incoherent mess, and even AI can't untangle it because the confusion gets embedded into its own context.

We have a term for this and it is called "Comprehension Debt" [0] [1].

[0] https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08942

[1] https://medium.com/@addyosmani/comprehension-debt-the-hidden...

在 HN 中查看
danielvaughn 2026/04/08 01:35
我不确定我是否同意该术语的适用。据我了解，理解债只是您链接的 arxiv 论文中提到的依赖陷阱。这意味着人工智能可能写了一些连贯或不连贯的东西，但作为人类评估者，你几乎没有办法判断它。因为你太依赖它了，代码的范围已经超出了手动阅读的可行性。

当我谈论语无伦次的混乱时，我谈论的是不同的东西。我的意思是，随着代码库的增长和成熟，微妙的细节和假设自然会发生变化。但人工智能并不总是清理表达这些先前假设的代码。这些问题使得人工智能本身变得非常混乱。这对于接触相同代码库的开发团队来说尤其危险。

我不能在这里分享太多细节，但我最近遇到了一些个人经历​​：我们的平台中有 ABC 功能。最终，另一位开发人员介入，不同意该实现，并将其某些方面组合成新功能 XYZ。两者都是人工智能生成的。应该发生的情况是，功能 ABC 已从代码中删除或重构为 XYZ。但事实并非如此，所以现在代码库有两个几乎相同的模块 ABC 和 XYZ。如果您要求 Claude 编辑该功能，则您可以以 50/50 的比例选择它所选择的目标，即使功能 ABC 现在已失效、无法访问代码。

您可能会说解决上述问题很容易，但如果您过度依赖人工智能或不小心，这些不一致在代码库中会变得相当多且不可持续。这就是为什么我说预先拥有超清晰的愿景很重要，因为它可以减少这种定向流失。

I'm not sure I agree the term applies. Comprehension debt, as I understand it, is just the dependency trap mentioned in that arxiv paper you linked. It means that the AI might have written something coherent or not, but you as a human evaluator have little means to judge it. Because you've relied on it too much and the scope of the code has exceeded the feasibility of reading it manually.

When I talk about an incoherent mess, I'm talking about something different. I mean that as the codebase grows and matures, subtle details and assumptions naturally shift. But the AI isn't always cleaning up the code that expressed those prior assumptions. These issues compound to the point that the AI itself gets very confused. This is especially dangerous for teams of developers touching the same codebase.

I can't share too much detail here, but some personal experience I ran into recently: we had feature ABC in our platform. Eventually another developer came in, disagreed with the implementation, and combined some aspects of it into a new feature XYZ. Both were AI generated. What _should_ have happened is that feature ABC was deleted from the code or refactored into XYZ. But it wasn't, so now the codebase has two nearly identical modules ABC and XYZ. If you ask Claude to edit the feature, you've got a 50/50 shot on which one it chooses to target, even though feature ABC is now dead, unreachable code.

You might say that resolving the above issue is easy, but these inconsistencies become quite numerous and unsustainable in a codebase if you lean on AI too much, or aren't careful. This is why I say that having a super clear vision up front is important, because it reduces this kind of directional churn.

在 HN 中查看
all2 2026/04/08 02:14
> 这就是为什么我说预先拥有超清晰的愿景很重要，因为它可以减少这种定向流失。

我正在起草一个项目的第六或第七稿。自一月底以来，我一直在挑剔这件事；我不断地重新开始，因为随着我的继续，核心抽象变得越来越清晰。人工智能在这个发现过程中表现出色，因为它可以更快地加快迭代速度。我知道当我不再清楚地了解它所做的工作时，它就会开始陷入混乱。对我来说，这表明我拥有和传达的某些心理模型不够精确。

> This is why I say that having a super clear vision up front is important, because it reduces this kind of directional churn.

I'm on my 6th or 7th draft of a project. I've been picking away at this thing since the end of January; I keep restarting because the core abstractions get clearer and clearer as I go. AI has been great in this discovery process because it speeds iteration much more quickly. I know its starting to drift into a mess when I no longer have a clear grasp of the work its doing. To me, this indicates that some mental model I had and communicated was not sufficiently precise.

Yep, for sure. Restarting is the right choice IMO, it's way easier than trying to untangle from a previous iteration.
在 HN 中查看
crystal_revenge 2026/04/08 00:46
> ...让人工智能得到有效利用。

我一直着迷于 巨大的 利用人工智能产生成功结果的个人能力存在差异。我一直认为人工智能的好处之一是“任何人都可以做到这一点”。然后我意识到我接触过很多人 不 他们真正理解他们想要解决的问题，并且有一些非理性的信念，认为他们可以让人工智能暴力解决问题。

对我来说，我什至不使用更强大的模型（仅使用 Sonnet 4.6），并且还没有一个项目在短时间内取得相当成功。这包括面试的分级实时编码示例，因此至少有一些客观的衡量标准来证明这些示例是否有效。

奇怪的是，我发现传统的软件工程师，尤其是经验丰富的软件工程师，通常是 最糟糕的 在取得成功时。他们经常对待与代理人的合作 也 就像软件工程一样，最终会构建出糟糕的软件，而不是解决核心问题的有用解决方案。

> ... for AI to be used effectively.

I'm continually fascinated by the huge differences in individual ability to produce successful results with AI. I always assumed that one of the benefits of AI was "anyone can do this". Then I realized a lot of people I interact with don't really understand the problem they're trying to solve all that well, and have some irrational belief that they can get AI to brute force their way to a solution.

For me I don't even use the more powerful models (just Sonnet 4.6) and have yet to have a project not come out fairly successful in a short period of time. This includes graded live coding examples for interviews, so there is at least some objective measurement that these are functional.

Strangely I find traditional software engineers, especially experienced ones, are generally the worst at achieving success. They often treat working with an agent too much like software engineering and end up building bad software rather than useful solutions to the core problem.

在 HN 中查看
gopher_space 2026/04/08 01:07
> 我一直认为人工智能的好处之一是“任何人都可以做到这一点”。然后我意识到与我互动的很多人并不真正理解他们试图很好地解决的问题

自 90 年代以来，我经历了几次“任何人都可以做到这一点”的顿悟，并逐渐意识到完整的陈述应该是“如果他们关心问题空间，任何人都可以做到这一点”。

> I always assumed that one of the benefits of AI was "anyone can do this". Then I realized a lot of people I interact with don't really understand the problem they're trying to solve all that well

I've been through a handful of "anyone can do this" epiphanies since the 90s and have come to realize the full statement should be "anyone can do this if they care about the problem space".

在 HN 中查看
lamasery 2026/04/08 01:16
我在工作中拥有的“人工智能”工具（并且被强制使用，并带有使用情况跟踪）并不是像那些在自己的时间进行实验的人可能拥有的那样一个广阔的选择领域，所以我坚持使用他们给我的任何东西。这些项目是棕地项目，与晦涩的行业特定系统集成，带有大量访问控制阻止程序，已经在进行中，并具有近期功能完成预期，几乎没有时间回去填写法学硕士需要良好运作的内容（例如，广泛的测试套件），并且 不能 破坏他们需要交互的各种数据库，其中大多数仅作为生产实例存在。

我确信我可以将一些简单的 SaaS 产品与我在周末定义的目标和功能组合在一起，这些工具全部由我自己完成（也没有通信/协调开销！）。我的意思是，对于大量的潜在产品，在过去 15 年多的时间里，我可以只用 Rails 和一些 gems 来实现，而不需要法学硕士，但现在我可以用 Typescript、Rust 或 Go 等来实现。与法学硕士，无论其价值如何。在工作中，由于受到完全不同的限制，结果远没有那么引人注目，我什至无法做到 试图 应用一些（据称）最高效的处理这些事情的模式。

与此同时，法学硕士正在使所有与代码相关的东西（例如幻灯片、图表和票务跟踪器）变得极其垃圾。

[编辑] 实际上，我认为这个问题是“为什么 Rails 在新建小型团队或单独项目中的生产力极大提升没有转化为所有可能相关领域的生产力大幅提高的开发，法学硕士如何才能做得更好？”例如，我希望看到一组博学的法学硕士助推者的演讲。不是以一种卑鄙的巨魔方式，我的意思是他们对为什么结果可能会有所不同的探索对我来说实际上很有趣。

"AI" tools I've got at work (and am mandated to use, complete with usage tracking) aren't a wide-open field of options like what someone experimenting on their own time might have, so I'm stuck with whatever they give me. The projects are brown-field, integrate with obscure industry-specific systems, are heavy with access-control blockers, are already in-flight with near-term feature completion expectations that leave little time for going back and filling in the stuff LLMs need to operate well (extensive test suites, say), and must not wreck the various databases they need to interact with, most of which exist only as a production instance.

I'm sure I could hack together some simple SaaS products with goals and features I'm defining myself in a weekend with these tools all on my own (no communication/coordination overhead, too!), though. I mean for an awful lot of potential products I could do that with just Rails and some gems and no LLM any time I liked over the last 15+ years or whatever, but now I could do it in Typescript or Rust or Go et c. with LLMs, for whatever that's worth. At work, with totally different constraints, the results are far less dramatic and I can't even feasibly attempt to apply some of the (reputedly) most-productive patterns of working with these things.

Meanwhile, LLMs are making all the code-adjacent stuff like slide decks, diagrams, and ticket trackers, incredibly spammy.

[EDIT] Actually, I think the question "why didn't Rails' extreme productivity boost in greenfield tiny-team or solo projects translate into vastly-more-productive development across all sectors where it might have been relevant, and how will LLMs do significantly better than that?" is one I'd like to see, say, a panel of learned LLM boosters address. Not in a shitty troll sort of way, I mean their exploration of why it might play out differently would actually be interesting to me.

crystal_revenge 2026/04/08 03:45
> 这些项目属于棕地，与晦涩的行业特定系统集成，带有大量访问控制拦截器

在这些情况下，我见过代理解决方案执行以下操作 最好的。我最成功、最具影响力的项目一直在工作中，让多个“晦涩的行业特定系统”相互交谈，从而解锁大量项目工作。

> The projects are brown-field, integrate with obscure industry-specific systems, are heavy with access-control blockers

These are cases where I've seen agentic solutions perform the best. My most successful and high impact projects have been at work, getting multiple "obscure industry-specific systems" talking to each other in ways that unblocks an incredible amount of project work.

在 HN 中查看
QuadmasterXLII 2026/04/08 01:06
如果你解决的每个项目都取得了成功，那么你就永远无法解决实际上不可能的问题，这是你应用于潜在问题的任何预过滤器的属性。鉴于您的预过滤器没有误报，缺失的主要信息是它有多少误报。
If every project you have tackled has come out successful, then you are managing to never tackle a problem that is secretly literally impossible, which is a property of whatever prefilter you are applying to potential problems. Given that your prefilter has no false positives, the main bit of missing information is how many false negatives it has.

pegasus 2026/04/08 01:16
> 面试的评分实时编码示例

是的，对于那些你可以放松并相信氛围的人。对于复杂的软件项目，你需要那些软件工程能力，否则你最终会陷入棘手的混乱。

> graded live coding examples for interviews

Yeah, for those you can just relax and trust the vibes. It's for complex software projects you need those software engineering chops, otherwise you end up with a intractable mess.

在 HN 中查看
crystal_revenge 2026/04/08 03:53
如果这是一个复杂的软件项目，您需要问的第一个问题是“这真的需要吗？ 软件 到底有没有？”

老实说，这是大多数传统工程师陷入困境的地方。他们不断地用新工具解决老问题并感到沮丧。我同意代理并不是构建“复杂软件项目”的好方法，但我认为“复杂软件项目”最好解决的问题空间正在迅速缩小。

我有多个供应商试图向我的团队出售一种产品，我们可以在一个下午内构建自己的核心功能。我们不 需要 该功能可以扩展到多个用户、满足各种需求、适应新的用例：我们不打算用它建立一家 SaaS 公司，我们只需要解决一个简单的问题。

但这些评论是轶事宝库，恰恰证明了我的观点。

If it's for a complex software project the first question you need to ask is "does this really need to be software at all?"

Honestly this is where most traditional engineers get stuck. They keep attacking the old problem with new tools and being frustrated. I agree that agents are not a great way to build "complex software projects" but I think the problem space that is best solved by a "complex software project" is rapidly shrinking.

I've had multiple vendors try to sell my team a product that we can build the core functionality of ourselves in an afternoon. We don't need that functionality to scale to multiple users, server a variety of needs, be adaptable to new use cases: we're not planning to build a SaaS company with it, we just need a simple problem solved.

But these comments are a treasure trove of anecdotes proving exactly my point.

在 HN 中查看
alfalfasprout 2026/04/08 01:04
> 奇怪的是，我发现传统的软件工程师，尤其是经验丰富的软件工程师，通常最难取得成功。他们经常将与代理的合作视为软件工程，最终会构建出糟糕的软件，而不是解决核心问题的有用解决方案。

这感觉有点像稻草人。如果你自己不是工程师，你如何评价它是一个糟糕的软件？对你来说什么才是成功？

如果说有什么不同的话，那就是人工智能工具表明，很多人对构建软件抱有傲慢态度。非工程师相信他们正在创造成功的工作，却没有意识到这是一个定时炸弹解决方案的表面。

> Strangely I find traditional software engineers, especially experienced ones, are generally the worst at achieving success. They often treat working with an agent too much like software engineering and end up building bad software rather than useful solutions to the core problem.

This feels a bit like a strawman. How do you assess it to be bad software without being an engineer yourself? What constitutes successful for you?

If anything, AI tools have revealed that a lot of people have hubris about building software. With non-engineers believing they're creating successful work without realizing it's a facade of a solution that's a ticking time bomb.

在 HN 中查看
crystal_revenge 2026/04/08 03:43
> 自己不是工程师？

我什么时候说过我不是软件工程师了？我有软件工程背景（我写过相当成功的软件书籍），我还做了很多其他人们往往认为更有价值的事情。

> 对您来说什么才是成功？

我需要解决的问题解决了吗？我不确定你还能采取什么其他措施。老实说，人们确实误解了如何使用代理。如果您的目标是“构建软件”，那么您就会遇到麻烦，如果您的目标是“解决问题”，那么您就会更了解这些工具最有效的工作方式。

> without being an engineer yourself?

When did I say I'm not a software engineer? I have a software engineering background (I've written reasonably successful books on software), I've just done a lot of other stuff as well that people tend to find more valuable.

> What constitutes successful for you?

The problem I need to solve is solved? I'm not sure what other measure you could have. Honestly, people really misunderstand how to use agents. If you're aim is to "build software" you're going to get in trouble, if your aim is to "solve problems" then you're more aligned with where these tools work most effectively.

在 HN 中查看
socalgal2 2026/04/08 02:31
但这不是暂时的情况吗？

今天：要求人工智能“做事”，人工审核，因为不信任人工智能

明天：让人工智能“做事”

我的人工智能之旅才刚刚开始。没过多久，我就从每月 17 美元的克劳德计划升级到每月 100 美元的计划，而且我很快就会选择每月 200 美元的计划。这是针对爱好项目的。

目前，我正在审查我正在处理的大部分代码，并且我也进行了测试并审查了这些代码。但是，看到它有多好（有时），我可以想象一个未来，人工智能本身既具有技术能力又具有品味，我可以说“也许我是一个编辑照片的应用程序”，它会吐出一个用户友好的 Photoshop 克隆，具有良好的用户体验。

我们已经在音乐中看到了这一点——它能够吐出“Bangers”。它还要多久才能推出热门浪漫喜剧、犯罪节目、食谱、应用程序？我不认为答案是“从不”。我认为答案更有可能是在 N 年之后，其中 N 可能是个位数。

Isn't this a temporary situation though.

Today: Ask AI to "do the thing", manual review because don't trust the AI

Tomorrow: Ask AI to "do the thing"

I'm just getting started on my AI journey. It didn't take long before I upgraded from the $17 a month claude plan to the $100 a month plan and I can see myself picking the $200 a month plan soon. This is for hobby projects.

At the moment I'm reviewing most of the code for what I'm working on, and I have tests and review those too. But, seeing how good it is (sometimes), I can imagine a future where the AI itself has both the tech chops and the taste and I can just say "Maybe me an app to edit photos" and it will spit out a user friendly clone of photoshop with good UX.

We already kind of see this with music - it's able to spit out "Bangers". How long until it can spit out hit rom-coms, crime shows, recipes, apps? I don't think the answer is "never". I think more likely the answer is in N years where N is probably a single digit.

在 HN 中查看
danielvaughn 2026/04/08 02:36
不，我不认为这是暂时的。随着人工智能变得越来越强大，我们只会要求它做更困难的事情。在一定程度的复杂性中，“做事”是不够的。我们永远不会达到人工智能可以从简单的人类请求中推断出大量细微差别的地步，这意味着人类始终需要能够准确地描述他们想要的东西。这一直是软件开发人员的核心技能，我认为这种情况不会改变。
No, I don't think it is temporary. As AI becomes more powerful, we'll simply ask it to do more difficult things. There's a level of complexity where "do the thing" is insufficient. We'll never be at a place where AI can infer vast amounts of nuance from simple human requests, which means that humans will always need to be able to describe precisely what they want. This has always been the core skill for software developers, and I just don't see that changing.
在 HN 中查看
drivebyhooting 2026/04/08 05:25
你相信现在的初级开发人员永远无法超越你吗？

为什么人工智能不能做同样的事情？

Do you believe a junior developer now will never surpass you?

Why couldn’t AI do the same?

在 HN 中查看
danielvaughn 2026/04/08 09:58
这不是是否超越我的问题。在某些方面它已经做到了——我看到 Claude Code 吐出很长的终端命令，这是我在 15 年职业生涯中从未见过的。

问题是人工智能是否会变得足够好，能够神奇地推断出未提供的信息。

例如，我有一个创办逐项物理存储公司的想法。我们永远不会达到我可以简单地说“嘿人工智能，创建逐项物理存储公司所需的所有软件”的地步。这并不是因为人工智能不会继续改进，而是因为该声明中没有足够的细节来理解我的意思。太模糊了。我确信明天的人工智能可以很好地猜测我的意思，但它捕捉到我的视觉的机会实际上是 0%。

It's not a matter of whether it surpasses me. In some respects it already has - I watch Claude Code spitting out long terminal commands that I've never even seen in my 15 year career.

The question is whether AI will ever become good enough to magically infer information where none is provided.

For instance, I've had this startup idea for an itemized physical storage company. We'll never reach a point where I can simply say "Hey AI, create all the software necessary for an itemized physical storage company". It's not because AI won't continue to improve, it's because there's literally not enough detail in that statement to understand what I mean. It's too vague. I'm sure the AI of tomorrow could do a pretty good job in guessing what I mean by it, but the chance of it capturing my vision is literally 0%.

在 HN 中查看
CharlieDigital 2026/04/08 00:16 

    > One of the most useful things about AI is also one of the most humbling: it reveals how clear your own judgment actually is. If your critique stays vague, your taste is still underdeveloped. If your critique becomes precise, your judgment is stronger than the model output. You can then use the model well instead of being led by it.
我发现团队在代理编码方面犯了错误：他们从现有代码库的逆向工程文档开始。

这是一个错误。

相反，正确的思路是：“什么会 完美的 代码是什么样的？”然后向法学硕士细致地描述什么是“完美”来塑造生成的每一行。

对于某些人来说，这个练习很难掌握，因为他们从来没有思考过构造良好的代码或架构是什么样子的；他们没有“品味”，因此没有能力精确地规定“完美”的框架（是的，存在一些反映品味的主观性）。

    > One of the most useful things about AI is also one of the most humbling: it reveals how clear your own judgment actually is. If your critique stays vague, your taste is still underdeveloped. If your critique becomes precise, your judgment is stronger than the model output. You can then use the model well instead of being led by it.
Something I find that teams get wrong with agentic coding: they start by reverse engineering docs from an existing codebase.

This is a mistake.

Instead, the right train of thought is: "what would perfect code look like?" and then meticulously describe to the LLM what "perfect" is to shape every line that gets generated.

This exercise is hard for some folks to grasp because they've never thought much about what well-constructed code or architectures looks like; they have no "taste" and thus no ability to precisely dictate the framework for "perfect" (yes, there is some subjectivity that reflects taste).

我认为这违背了许多开发人员对人工智能的期望（澄清一下，不是我）。

> Instead, the right train of thought is: "what would perfect code look like?" and then meticulously describe to the LLM what "perfect" is to shape every line that gets generated.

I think this goes against what a lot of developers want AI to be (not me, to be clear).

在 HN 中查看
CharlieDigital 2026/04/08 00:25
我是从团队的角度来看这个问题的。

有了正确的文档，我可以将每个技能水平的每个开发人员提升到最低“底线”，并影响致力于使其更接近“完美”的每一行代码。

我不会写每个提示，因此仍然存在一些变化，但这种方法通过使初始生成过程尽可能接近“完美”，以非常小的开销为我们提供了非常高质量的 PR。

I'm looking at it from a team perspective.

With the right docs, I can lift every developer of every skill level up to a minimum "floor" and influence every line of code that gets committed to move it closer to "perfect".

I'm not writing every prompt so there is still some variation, but this approach has given us very high quality PRs with very minimal overhead by getting the initial generation passes as close to "perfect" as reasonably possible.

aaaronic 2026/04/08 01:01
当同事拒绝阅读我（个人，故意）写的内容（他们要求人工智能进行总结），然后告诉人工智能写下他们的回应时，这确实让我感到惊讶，而它绝对是臃肿的，并且充满了对我原始文档的误解。

如果他们不愿意阅读我所付出的努力，为什么我应该阅读那些考虑不周且冗长的回复呢？我真的不想让我的人工智能与你的人工智能争论，但这就是他们告诉我我应该做的......

It does amaze me when colleagues refuse to read what I (personally, deliberately) wrote (they ask AI to summarize), but then tell AI to write their response and it's absolutely bloated and full of misconceptions around my original document.

If they aren't willing to read what I put effort into, why should I be expected to read the ill-conceived and verbose response? I really don't want to get into a match of my AI arguing with your AI, but that's what they've told me I should be doing...

switchbak 2026/04/08 02:24
我和一位经理之间一直存在问题，他以克劳德指导的公关形式做出回应。毫无疑问是受到混乱提示的驱使。总是充满问题，从来没有真正解决问题，总是在这个过程中添加大量额外的废话。

上面存在着努力的不对称，当与权力的不对称相结合时——这是一个非常糟糕的组合，而且我不认为我是唯一这样的人。

我很高兴看到这个论坛对复杂性带来的巨大成本的认识，但我认为这还没有上升到管理层面。

I've been having ongoing issues with a manager who responds in the form of Claude guided PRs. Undoubtedly driven from confused prompts. Always full of issues, never actually solving the problem, always adding HEAPS of additional nonsense in the process.

There's an asymmetry of effort in the above, and when combined with the power asymmetry - that's a really bad combo, and I don't think I'm alone.

I'm glad to see the appreciation of the enormous costs of complexity on this forum, but I don't think that has ascended to the managerial level.

CharlieDigital 2026/04/08 02:40 

    > ...a manager who responds in the form of Claude guided PRs
我认为在这个即将到来的时代，开发人员的工作是开发非工程师可以胜任构建的系统，而不会破坏产品或产生无法维护的代码。

在我目前的角色中，我已从主导 IC 转向构建供其他 IC 和非 IC 使用的系统。

从我的角度来看，如果我能为代理提供正确的护栏，那么使用任何代理的任何人都将生成围绕更高质量基线合并的代码。 我现在的大部分 IC 工作都是围绕这个方向进行的。

    > ...a manager who responds in the form of Claude guided PRs
I think the job of a dev in this coming era is to produce the systems by which non-engineers can build competently and not break prod or produce unmaintainable code.

In my current role, I have shifted from lead IC to building the system that is used by other IC's and non-IC's.

From my perspective, if I can provide the right guardrails to the agent, then anyone using any agent will produce code that is going to coalesce around a higher baseline of quality. Most of my IC work now is aligned on this directionality.

aaaronic 2026/04/08 01:06
我曾在太多大型代码库中工作过，其中没有人可以指向任何_单个文件或类_并将其标记为“正确”（“正确的方式”），但当缺乏“北极星”意味着代码库充满了重叠的、零碎的模式时，管理层感到惊讶，而这些模式能够幸运地一起工作。
I've worked in too many large codebases where no one can point to any _single file or class_ and label it "correct," ("the right way") yet management is amazed when the lack of a "North Star" means the codebase is full of overlapping, piecemeal patterns that are lucky to work together at all.

CharlieDigital 2026/04/08 01:18
这就是为什么团队需要有“品味”的人来决定惯用的方法，以及为什么法学硕士（以这种方式使用时）可以提高质量底线和一致性基线。
That's why the team needs someone with "taste" to dictate the idiomatic way to do it and why LLMs (when used this way) can raise the floor of quality and baseline of consistency.
在 HN 中查看
dist-epoch 2026/04/08 00:29
> 相反，正确的思路是：“完美的代码是什么样的？”

这就是经典的第二系统效果——“现在我们知道我们想要什么了，让我们从头开始重写它”。然后你就抛弃了所有来之不易的教训。

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-system_effect

> Instead, the right train of thought is: "what would perfect code look like?"

That's the classic 2nd-system effect - "let's rewrite it from scratch, now that we know what we want". And you throw away all the hard-learned lessons.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-system_effect

在 HN 中查看
CharlieDigital 2026/04/08 00:57
事实并非如此；您误解了第二系统效应这个术语。

    > The general tendency is to over-design the second system, using all the ideas and frills that were cautiously sidetracked on the first one.  The result, as Ovid says is a "big pile".  For example, consider the IBM 709 architecture, later embodied in the 7090.  This is an upgrade, a second system for the very successful and clean 704.  The operation set is so rich and profuse that only about half of it was regularly used.  (p.55)
    > 
    > The second-system effect has another manifestation somewhat different from pure functional embellishment.  That is a tendency to refine techniques whose very existence has been made obsolete by changes in basic system assumptions. (p.56)
恰恰相反：通过明确规定什么是正确的、完美的和标准的 这 在代码库中，我们实现了非常高的一致性和质量，几乎没有“修饰”和多余的内容，因为法学硕士遵循一组精心策划的指令，而不是团队中每个开发人员的突发奇想。

建议你重新读一下布鲁克斯所说的“第二系统效应”是什么意思。

Not really the case; you're misunderstanding the term second system effect.

    > The general tendency is to over-design the second system, using all the ideas and frills that were cautiously sidetracked on the first one.  The result, as Ovid says is a "big pile".  For example, consider the IBM 709 architecture, later embodied in the 7090.  This is an upgrade, a second system for the very successful and clean 704.  The operation set is so rich and profuse that only about half of it was regularly used.  (p.55)
    > 
    > The second-system effect has another manifestation somewhat different from pure functional embellishment.  That is a tendency to refine techniques whose very existence has been made obsolete by changes in basic system assumptions. (p.56)
It's the exact opposite: by explicitly dictating what is correct, perfect, and standard in this codebase, we achieve very high consistency and quality with very little "embellishment" and excess because the LLM is following a set of highly curated instructions rather than the whims of each developer on the team.

Suggest that you re-read what Brooks meant by "second system effect".

在 HN 中查看
subhobroto 2026/04/08 02:24
> 相反，正确的思路是：“完美的代码是什么样的？”然后向法学硕士细致地描述什么是“完美”的塑造生成的每条线。

我认为不存在完美的代码。

代码是自动化的——它使人类的努力自动化，而人类本身也有错误，因此并不完美。

因此，只要代码达到或超过人类的输出，它就“足够好”并满足期望。这就是典型客户所关心的。

顾客会很高兴地选择在他们的预算范围内用防水布和塑料棒制成的帐篷， 现在 当外面下雨的时候，在一个建筑奇迹上，这个建筑奇迹将在未来的某个时候以某个未知的价格点出售。

换句话说，我不认为你构建了 CharlieAI CharlieGPT 今天，其中与 ChatGPT 的唯一区别因素是 CharlieGPT 是使用以下代码编写的 完美的代码，你就会有任何有意义的优势。

我还没有看到 任何 有证据表明，在其他条件相同的情况下，一家公司仅凭借卓越的代码就比另一家公司更具优势。

事实上，我有压倒性的证据表明，拥有更好代码的公司会屈服于并消失，而与代码很少的公司相比， 如果有的话 代码，因为这些钱被投资于更好的客户发现、细分和分析（“什么 应该 我们建造？”，“如果我们建造的话 一 会给我们的客户带来不公平优势的事情是什么 那 事物？”）

软件历史充满了完美的操作系统、编辑器、框架、协议，但随着时间的推移，这些都消失了，因为一个被证明是劣质的选择赢得了市场份额。

您现在正在使用软件控制的 SMPS 为您的设备供电。你对代码的质量一无所知。您所关心的只是 SMPS 是否会过早耗尽您的电池电量并不必要地加热您的设备。如此高效、 低开销 控制系统是使用抽象模块编写的。它是 更有可能 该控制系统充满了 哥特人 反复违反 DRY 会让完美主义者颤抖和哭泣。

> Instead, the right train of thought is: "what would perfect code look like?" and then meticulously describe to the LLM what "perfect" is to shape every line that gets generated.

I don't think there's perfect code.

Code is automation - it automates human effort and humans themselves have error, hence not perfect.

So as long as code meets or exceeds the human output, it's "good enough" and meets expectations. That's what a typical customer cares about.

A customer will happily choose a tent made of tarp and plastic sticks that's available at their budget, right now when it's raining outside, over an architectural marvel that will be available sometime in the future at some unknown pricepoint.

Put another way, I don't think if you built CharlieAI CharlieGPT today, where the only differentiating factor over ChatGPT was that CharlieGPT was written using perfect code, you would have any meaningful edge.

I am yet to see any evidence where everything else being equal, one company had an edge over another simply due to superior code.

Infact, I have overwhelming evidence of companies that had better code succumb and vanish against companies that had very little, if any code, because those dollars were instead invested in better customer discovery, segmentation and analytics ("what should we build?", "if we did one thing that would give our customers an unfair advantage, what would be that thing?")

Software history is full of perfect OS, editors, frameworks, protocols that is lost over time because a provably inferior option won marketshare.

You are using a software controlled SMPS to power your device right now. You have 0 idea what the quality of that code is. All you care about is whether that SMPS drains your battery prematurely and heats up your device unnecessarily. It's extremely unlikely that such an efficient, low overhead control system was written using well abstracted modules. It's more likely that control system is full of gotos and repeated violations of DRY that would make a perfectionist shudder and cry.

在 HN 中查看
CharlieDigital 2026/04/08 02:43 

    > I don't think there's perfect code
请注意，我在文本中使用了“完美”。 在这种情况下，这意味着它按照我们的标准指南通过了人工公关审查，并且需要最少的反馈/纠正。

    > So as long as code meets or exceeds the human output, it's "good enough" and meets expectations. That's what a typical customer cares about.
既然“完美”就是“免费”，为什么还要满足于此呢？ 我理解这种二分法，因为编写“完美”代码需要更昂贵、更有经验的人力资源或更多时间，所以你满足于“足够好”；但现在情况不再是这样了，不是吗？ “完美”的成本可能只比糟糕的高几分之一美分。

你只需要准确地描述LLM的“完美”是什么，而不是让它回归到训练集的平均值。 现在编写糟糕的代码和“完美”的代码之间确实没有成本差异；这只是你向法学硕士描述“完美”的能力有多强的问题。

例如，我们非常特别希望我们的代理使用 C# 元组返回类型为返回超过 1 个值的私有方法编写代码，而不是创建一个类。 元组返回类型是堆栈分配的值类型，并且具有默认的析构函数。 我们还总是希望每次都使用命名元组字段，因为它可以消除人类的歧义并提高代理重新阅读代码时的效率。

我们希望代码使用模式匹配和切换表达式（不是 `switch-case`），因为它们有助于在编译时强制执行详尽的检查并使代码更加简洁。

如果我们只是提前告诉代理这些规则，我们每次都会得到“完美”、一致的代码。 能够做到这一点需要“品味”并理解为什么以一种方式编写代码或使用特定的语言结构或特定的设计模式是“正确”的方式。

    > I don't think there's perfect code
Note I used "perfect" in my text. In this context, meaning it passes human PR reviews following our standard guidelines with minimal feedback/correction required.

    > So as long as code meets or exceeds the human output, it's "good enough" and meets expectations. That's what a typical customer cares about.
Why settle for this when "perfect" is "free"? I understand this dichotomy when writing "perfect" code requires more expensive, more experienced human resources or more time so you settle for "good enough"; but this is no longer the case, is it? The cost of "perfect" is only perhaps a few fractions of a cent higher than shitty.

You only need to accurately describe what "perfect" is to the LLM instead of allowing it to regress to the mean of its training set. There really is no cost difference between writing shitty code and "perfect" code now; its just a matter of how good you are at describing "perfect" to the LLM.

For example, we very specifically want our agents to write code using C# tuple return types for private methods that return more than 1 value instead of creating a class. The tuple return type is a stack allocated value type and has a default deconstructor. We also always want to use named tuple fields every time because it removes ambiguity for humans and increases efficiency for agents when re-reading the code.

We want the code to make use of pattern matching and switch expressions (not `switch-case`) because they help enforce exhaustive checks at compile time and make the code more terse.

If we simply tell the agent these rules ahead of time, we get "perfect", consistent code each time. Being able to do so requires "taste" and understanding why writing code one way or using a specific language construct or a specific design pattern is the "right" way.

在 HN 中查看
subhobroto 2026/04/08 04:18
> 现在编写糟糕的代码和“完美”代码之间确实没有成本差异；这只是你向法学硕士描述“完美”的能力有多强的问题。

结果与前因不一致。这是一个表现上的矛盾（如果输出确实是“免费”的，操作员的技能将是一个零值变量 - 然而，通过需要技能，你承认成本），正如我在下面证明的那样

> “完美”的成本可能只比糟糕的高几分之一美分。

您的成本模型是否考虑了规范成本、审查成本以及审查失败或规范本身需要调整时所需的额外周期？

> 如果我们只是提前告诉代理这些规则，我们每次都会得到“完美”、一致的代码

不，在最简单的情况下，完美的成本只是将抽象链从实现（编码）向上移动到设计和规范。实际上，它还会拆分部分成本并将其转移到下游进行验证。

这不是什么特殊的、神奇的见解，我是在向你们重申特斯勒定律。

我还鼓励您阅读软件历史 - 几十年来，从 ER 和 UML 图中分离出完美工作的 CRUD 一直是微不足道的，不需要 LLM。洞察力在于理解为什么我们继续雇用廉价劳动力来生成 CRUD，而不是使用这些工具。

软件的成本始终在于弄清楚意图，而不是生成语法。

我希望 pg 在 HN 上更加活跃 - 我认为这是他希望创始人了解并分享他们（潜在）客户的痛点的原因之一。弄清楚意图的成本很高。错误的意图和最好的情况是一个枢轴。

> There really is no cost difference between writing shitty code and "perfect" code now; its just a matter of how good you are at describing "perfect" to the LLM.

The consequent is at odds with the antecedent. It's a performative contradiction (if the output were truly "free", the skill of the operator would be a zero-value variable - yet, by requiring skill, you acknowledge a cost) as I prove below

> The cost of "perfect" is only perhaps a few fractions of a cent higher than shitty.

Is your cost model accounting for the cost of specification, of review and additional cycles required if review fails or the specification itself needs to be adjusted?

> If we simply tell the agent these rules ahead of time, we get "perfect", consistent code each time

No, in the simplest case, your cost of perfection is simply moving up the chain of abstraction from implementation (coding) to design and specification. In reality it also splits and moves a part of that cost downstream to verification.

This isn't some special, magical insight I have, I'm reiterating Tesler's Law right back to you.

I also encourage you to read software history - for decades it has been trivial to split out perfectly working CRUD from an ER and UML diagram, no LLM necessary. The insight is understanding why we continue to hire cheap human labor to spit out CRUD instead of using those tools.

The cost of software is, and always has been, in the figuring out the intent, not the generation of syntax.

I wish pg was more active on HN - I expect this is one of the reasons why he wanted founders to have and share the painpoints of their (potential) customers. Figuring out the intent is expensive. Mistake the intent and the best case scenario is a pivot.

在 HN 中查看
CharlieDigital 2026/04/08 05:09 

    > I wish pg was more active on HN - I expect this is one of the reasons why he wanted founders to have and share the painpoints of their (potential) customers. Figuring out the intent is expensive. Mistake the intent and the best case scenario is a pivot.
你的错误在于你认为重点是只有工程师参与代码的生产。 事实上，重点是产品团队和最接近客户的人可以生成代码。 出于这个原因，我们的目标是创建一个框架，无论用户是工程人员还是产品人员，都可以在该框架上相对轻松且一致地生成“完美”代码。

    > Is your cost model accounting for the cost of specification
无论如何，这都是相同的成本。 LLM 不会自行生成代码；无论如何，一些操作员必须提供一些说明和规范，因此您最好给它一些好的说明和规范。 但在这里，我要指出的是，存在高水平的更广泛的一般指令，这些指令会产生一次性的规范成本（“总是写 这段代码是这样的”）。

    > I wish pg was more active on HN - I expect this is one of the reasons why he wanted founders to have and share the painpoints of their (potential) customers. Figuring out the intent is expensive. Mistake the intent and the best case scenario is a pivot.
Your mistake is that you think the point is that only engineers participate in the production of code. In fact, the point is that the product team and the people closest to the customer can generate the code. And for that reason, the goal is to produce a framework on top of which "perfect" code can be produced with relative ease and consistency regardless of whether the user is part of engineering or product.

    > Is your cost model accounting for the cost of specification
This is the same cost no matter what. The LLM does not generate code on its own; some operator must provide some instruction and specification regardless so you might as well give it good ones. But here, I would point out that there is a high level of broader general instructions that incur a one-time cost of specification ("Always write this code this way").

wallstop 2026/04/08 06:48
我不认为你真的需要一个工具，你可以添加类似“任务完成后，让一个子代理以对抗性方式审查工作。如果发现任何缺陷，无论多小，让另一个子代理实施发现的结果。循环重复此操作，直到所有子代理达成共识，即产品具有卓越的质量，没有缺陷”或类似于每个提示。每个子代理都有自己的、新鲜的上下文窗口。无需任何工具。
I don't think you really need a tool for that, you can just add something like "after the task is finished, have a subagent review the work in an adversarial fashion. If any defects, no matter how small are found, have another subagent implement the findings. Repeat this in a loop until all subagents achieve consensus that the product is of exceptional quality with no defects" or similar to each prompt. Each subagent gets its own, fresh, context window. No tooling required.

everybodyknows 2026/04/08 02:30
试图让我的人工智能鼻子达到标准——愿意分享你闻到的气味吗？ 对我来说是：

- 简短的陈述性句子，用高中词汇来陈述宏大而又模糊的主张：“当品味从氛围转向诊断时，它就会变得有用。”

- 缺乏对现实世界示例的引用（更不用说网络链接）。

Trying to bring my nose for AI up to standard -- care to share what you're smelling? For me it's:

- Short, declarative sentences, stating grandiose yet vague claims, in a high school vocabulary: "Taste becomes useful when it moves from vibe to diagnosis."

- Absence of references (let alone web links) to real-world examples.

- Em-dashes, gone. No semicolons, but 23 full colons. As instructed by prompt?

jdauriemma 2026/04/08 03:06
对此我要补充一点：

* 大量的有序和无序列表

* 段落 <= 3 句话

* _这不是 X，而是 Y_：“我们的目标不是让 AI 为你选择。我们的目标是建立一个更清晰的拒绝词汇表。” “最大的决定不是格式决定。而是方向性决定。”

* 很多 <h2> 打破了散文，如果你可以这样称呼它的话

* 设置语句，然后是冒号，然后是一句妙语：“人工智能和法学硕士很快改变了一件事：有能力的输出现在很便宜。”

人工智能生成的论文本质上是列表文章

To that I'd add:

* an abundance of ordered and unordered lists

* paragraphs are <= 3 sentences

* _it's not X, it's Y_: "The goal is not to let AI choose for you. The goal is to build a sharper rejection vocabulary." "The biggest decisions are not formatting decisions. They are directional decisions."

* a lot of <h2> breaking up the prose, if you can call it that

* setup statement, then a colon, then a punchline: "AI and LLMs have changed one thing very quickly: competent output is now cheap."

AI-generated essays are listicles at heart

janalsncm 2026/04/08 03:30
“这就是事情变得有趣的地方”这句话让我知道了。

法学硕士正在竭尽全力吸引您的注意力。它已经根据承包商评分的数百万个示例进行了调整。如何让一个相当平淡的话题变得有趣？首先告诉人们接下来的事情会很有趣。然后，将一个相当明显的观点膨胀成几个句子，使其成为段落状。

The “Here’s where things get interesting” sentence gave it away for me.

The LLM is desperately trying to keep your attention. It has been tuned with millions of examples graded by contractors. How do you spice up a fairly bland topic? Start by telling people that what follows will be interesting. Then, bloat a fairly obvious point into several sentences so that it is paragraph-shaped.

marcus_holmes 2026/04/08 09:42
我不确定这是否仅适用于法学硕士 - 大多数公司网站都非常相似。专业设计师很快就会学会不要太古怪或独特。

我认为这是人类的症状——我们的舒适区是“十分之七”的相似性和平淡的泥沼。我们是群居动物。 LLM 正好反映了这一点。

我不认为我们的羊群倾向会让我们选择古怪的异常值，即使这是非法学硕士输出的唯一区别特征。

I'm not sure this is only true of LLMs - most corporate sites are very similar. Professional designers learn very quickly not to be too quirky or unique.

I think this is symptomatic of humans - our comfort zone is the "7 out of 10" morass of similarity and blandness. We are herd animals. LLMs are just reflecting this.

And I don't think our tendency to herd will allow us to select the quirky outliers even if that's the only distinguishing characteristic of non-LLM output.

30minAdayHN 2026/04/08 01:47
我认为石英危机与手表市场的情况有相似之处。就像 Quartz 导致瑞士机芯衰落一样，LLM 将对开发者市场产生巨大影响。我假设，未来将会出现一个关注质量、品味、独特性等的微型细分市场，就像豪华手表制造商找到一个利基市场一样。我的观点是，这种“品味”或“品质”不会成为护城河。相反，它将是一个只有一小部分人会关心的利基市场。

（编辑：错别字）

I think there is a parallel to what happened to watch market with Quartz crisis. The same way Quartz has led to decline of Swiss movements, LLMs are going to have a huge effect on developer market. I hypothesize that in future there will be a micro segment which care about quality, taste, exclusivity etc the same way the luxury watch makers found a niche. My perspective is that this "taste" or "quality" will not be a moat. Instead, it will be a niche where only a small segment would care about it.

(edit: typos)

They are visibly displaying a complete lack of personal taste, instead wearing the SV equivalent of an outdated-cut, off-the-rack navy blue (or even black, LOL) business suit.

The joke is that the message "good taste is what matters now" is being delivered by someone apparently, in a specifically SV sort of way, with a deficit of good taste.

gmaster1440 2026/04/08 00:12
如果你确实有过惨痛的教训，那么为什么在设计、开发和更好的整体思考方面，更好的模型不继续发展并提高品味和洞察力呢？
If you're properly bitter-lesson-pilled then why wouldn't better models continue to develop and improve taste and discernment when it comes to design, development, and just better thinking overall?
在 HN 中查看
wavemode 2026/04/08 00:15
我认为这意味着 AGI（即比人类更聪明或更聪明的东西）的诞生，在这一点上许多人认为这是科幻小说。

> 惨痛的教训

“惨痛的教训”并不意味着通用人工智能即将到来，它只是说让人工智能自己学习比直接教它们东西能产生更好的结果。

I think that would imply the creation of AGI (i.e. something as intelligent or more intelligent than mankind), which many consider to be science fiction at this point.

> bitter-lesson-pilled

The "bitter lesson" doesn't imply that AGI is coming, all it says is that letting AIs learn on their own yields better results than directly teaching them things.

在 HN 中查看
gwern 2026/04/08 03:21
它们确实有所改进，但随着规模的扩大，我们看到的总体创造力和火花主要来自于预训练/参数大小的扩大，因此与人们在小型廉价模型中的数学/编码中理所当然的速度（和不断降低的成本）相比，它相当缓慢且昂贵。因此，人们对 GPT-4.5 的反应是：基于比例定律，它应该具有更好的品味和洞察力，但几乎普遍认为这是一个巨大的失败。它和最初的 GPT-3 论文发布时一样不受欢迎，因为人们看到放大 10 倍或 100 倍所带来的对数式收益并感到失望。 “仅此而已？！痛苦的教训或缩放给我带来了什么？ 最近？”

因此，您可以预期编码技能将继续超越本地法学硕士的品味。

They do improve, but the general creativity and sparkle we see with increasing scale comes mostly from scaling up pretraining/parameter-size, so it's quite slow and expensive compared to the speed (and decreasing cost) people have come to take for granted in math/coding in small cheap models. Hence the reaction to GPT-4.5: exactly as much better taste and discernment as it should have had based on scaling laws, yet regarded almost universally as a colossal failure. It was as unpopular as the original GPT-3 was when the paper was released, because people look at the log-esque gains from scaling up 10x or 100x and are disappointed. "Is that all?! What has the Bitter Lesson or scaling done for me lately?"

So, you can expect coding skills to continue to outpace the native LLM taste.

在 HN 中查看
gmaster1440 2026/04/08 03:36
我想我们基本上是同意的。你的观点（如果我没读错的话）是，品味和洞察力确实可以扩展，但收益来自于预训练/参数扩展，与较小模型在数学/编码中快速、廉价的胜利相比，这是缓慢且昂贵的。所以味道更多的是一个滞后的规模指标。它有所改进，但这是人们注意到的最后一件事，因为可衡量的东西正在领先。这也意味着品味并不是真正的护城河，只是商品化的时间较晚而已。
I think we're basically agreeing here. Your point (if I'm reading it right) is that taste and discernment do scale, but the gains come through pretraining/parameter scaling, which is slow and expensive compared to the fast, cheap wins in math/coding from smaller models. So taste is more of a lagging indicator of scale. it improves, but it's the last thing people notice because the benchmarkable stuff races ahead. Which also means taste isn't really a moat, just late to get commoditized.
在 HN 中查看
gwern 2026/04/08 04:26
我的观点更多的是，既然你可以预期味道的商品化会因为深层次的根本原因而落后，那么味道 做 充当护城河。只是也许一个比人们天真的期望的更弱，你将不得不疯狂地继续投资，以保持领先于慢慢赶上的法学硕士，而不是永久锁定，你可以懒惰地垄断无限期地滑行。 （我想起 Neal Stephenson 在《开始是命令行》中对开源软件与专有软件进行的 La Brea 柏油坑类比。）
My point is more that since you can expect taste's commoditization to lag behind for deep fundamental reasons, then taste does serve as a moat. Just perhaps a weaker one than one would naively expect, and where you will have to frantically keep investing in it to stay ahead of the LLMs slowly catching up, as opposed to a permanent lock-in you can lazily monopolistically coast on indefinitely. (I'm reminded of Neal Stephenson's La Brea tarpit analogy for open source vs proprietary software in _In The Beginning was the Commandline_.)
在 HN 中查看
gmaster1440 2026/04/08 04:52
很公平。我真的很喜欢柏油坑的比喻，但并不熟悉它。只要你愿意继续花费精力，你就可以比焦油上升的速度更快地退出，但随着时间的推移，回报可能会递减。
Fair enough. I really like the tarpit analogy, wasn't familiar with it. You can keep pulling your feet out faster than the tar rises, as long as you're willing to keep spending the energy, possibly with diminishing returns over time.

nonameiguess 2026/04/08 00:48
无论工具有多好，第三方工具永远无法成为产品的差异化因素，除非您以某种方式设法拥有独占访问权。否则，其他人都可以并且将会使用与您相同的工具。它更像是一个享乐的跑步机而不是护城河。
Regardless of how good the tools get, third-party tooling can never be a product differentiator unless you somehow manage to have exclusive access. Otherwise, everyone else out there can and will use the same tools you are. It's more a hedonic treadmill than a moat.
在 HN 中查看
LurkandComment 2026/04/08 00:35
考虑长期与短期的护城河：

速度和分发并不是长期的护城河，因为它们是人工智能可以在平台中利用的东西。最终，他们将在您的分销基础上共存，并以比您更低的成本提供服务。 如果在你以高估值退出之前它能坚持下去，那就是一个小问题……这是很多事情要做的。

味道：这很有趣。那里有一个争论。 长期保持很难，需要对新人才进行大量再投资

专有数据：是的，非常如此。

贸易工艺：你的新闪亮系统仍然必须遵守旧的笨重的现实世界系统的方法。 例如，法庭证据。调查方法。这将是针对特定行业的，但您会惊讶地发现有如此多的行业。 这是长期的。

那些拥有护城河的人应该专注于有意义的变革的短暂爆发，因为他们将严重依赖于获得对现有系统的信任。 在这些地方，更多的是信任正在发生的事情，而不是做得更快更好，所以你需要信任+更快和/或更好。

Think about moats in long term vs short term:

Speed and distribution aren't a long-run moat because they are something AI can canabalize in a platform. Eventually they will coexist on your distribution base and offer it at a lower cost than you. Its a mote if it holds up before you exit at a high valuation... which a lot are setup to do.

Taste: that's interesting. There is an argument there. It's hard to keep in the long-run and requires a lot of reinvestment in new talent

Proprietary data: Yes, very much so.

Trade Craft: Your new shiney system will still have to adhere to methods of of old clunky real world systems. Example, evidence for court. Methods for investigations. This is going to be industry specific, but you'd be surprised how many there are. This is long-term.

Those who have the moat should focus on short burts of meaningful changes as they will rely heavily on gaining trust in established systems. In those places its more about trusting whats going on than doing it faster and better, so you want trust + faster and/or better.

在 HN 中查看
hamasho 2026/04/08 09:05
当有人说“人工智能只需提出简单的提示就可以完成大部分工作”时，我真的很怀疑。

不要误会我的意思，我也使用人工智能来完成日常任务和我作为程序员的工作，它肯定可以帮助我更快地完成工作（有时几乎是即时的）。 但对于复杂或非常规的任务，仍然需要付出很大的努力。

当我听到“人工智能完成了我的大部分工作”时，我想到了 DOGE 员工，他们使用人工智能来识别“浪费金钱”。 他们所做的只是用非常懒惰的提示来询问人工智能，比如“列出 DEI 项目”，其中列出了政府资助的项目列表，包括简单的描述。 他们甚至没有提供 DEI 的含义。 他们只是砍掉了人工智能标记的所有项目。 我确信他们的“生产力”非常高。 他们可以通过一个提示“完成需要数天、数周或数月调查的工作”。

我还认为结果有很强的倾向将一个项目标记为 DEI，因为“这是 DEI 吗”是右翼网站上的种族主义者和厌恶女性主义者经常提出的问题，而答案往往是“是”，这可能会导致强烈的偏见。

[1] https://www.9news.com/article/news/politics/doge-chatgpt-dei...

I get really skeptical when someone says "AI does most of the work just by asking simple prompts."

Don't get me wrong, I use AI too for daily tasks and my job as a programmer, and it definitely helps me get the job done faster (sometimes it's almost instant). But it still requires a lot of effort for complex or unconventional tasks.

When I hear "AI does most of my job", I think of DOGE employees who use AI to identify "waste of money". All they do is ask AI with very lazy prompts like "list DEI projects" with the list of government sponsored projects including simple descriptions. They don't even provide what DEI means. And they just cut all projects the AI flagged. I'm sure their "productivity" is very high. They can "complete the job" that would require days, weeks, or months of investigation with a single prompt.

I also think the results have a strong tendency to flag a project as DEI, because "is this DEI" is a question often asked by racists and misogynists on right-wing websites, and often the answers are "Yes", and that likely causes a strong bias.

[1] https://www.9news.com/article/news/politics/doge-chatgpt-dei...

yawnxyz 2026/04/08 01:00
良好的判断力和努力一直是“真正的护城河”——在艺术、音乐、科学、食品、产品......

总是有办法“扁平化中间”——通过外包、使用预包装商品、工业化……

所以，是的，我们一直喜欢手工制作的精致的东西；中间从来没有“护城河”

但这并不意味着没有护城河你就不能过上好的生活

Good judgment and effort has always been the "real moat" - in arts, music, science, food, product...

There's always been ways to "flatten the middle" - by outsourcing, by using pre-packaged goods, with industrialization...

So yeah we've always loved handcrafted, exquisite things; there's never been a "moat" in middle

It doesn't mean you can't make a good living without a moat though

drob518 2026/04/08 01:25
在我看来，品味一直是最坚固的护城河之一，因为我们很难定义什么是好品味。当我们看到它时我们就知道它，但除了举例之外，我们无法真正笼统地描述它。我仍然记得保罗·格雷厄姆（Paul Graham）在《黑客与画家》中的一句话，他描述了雇用软件工程师的困难。他说他在接受采访后与一位同事交谈时说道（我是转述的），“我知道他会写代码。但是他有品味吗？”品味是我们都希望我们的同事、产品和服务拥有的东西，但定义它确实很困难。是的，我完全同意作者的观点，即在这个发电成本低廉的人工智能时代，这一点比以往任何时候都更加重要。
IMO, taste has always been one of the strongest moats because we struggle to define what good taste even is. We know it when we see it, but other than pointing to examples, we can’t really describe it in general terms. I still remember a line from Paul Graham’s Hackers and Painters where he was describing the difficulty of hiring software engineers. He says he was talking with a colleague after an interview and remarked (I’m paraphrasing), “I know he can write code. But does he have taste?” Taste is something we all want our colleagues and products and services to have, but defining it is really difficult. And yes, I fully agree with the writer that it’s important more than ever in this age of AI where generation is cheap.

jrm4 2026/04/08 00:30
它掩盖了更重要的一点，一项技术尚未了解。

品味可能有点重要，因为它有助于实现真正重要的事情，即利益攸关。

而且，有了正确的游戏皮肤，你甚至不需要“品味”。你只需要现实生活中的后果，而我们在技术方面做得还不够。

It buried the more important point, one tech hasn't learned yet.

Taste may be kind of important because it helps toward the truly important thing, which is skin-in-the-game.

But also, with the right skin-in-the-game, you don't even need "taste." You just need real life consequences, which we don't do enough in tech.

lostathome 2026/04/08 01:14
我已经不同意第一句话：有能力的产出并不便宜。至少如果定义为最终产品的话。

- 想想科学研究吧。获得大量数据分析结果并不便宜。

- 即使是氛围编码也很困难：你需要认真思考你想要什么。

现在比较便宜的是一些积木。我们刚刚对构建块有了新的定义。但放置积木仍然很困难。

I already disagree with the first line: competent output is not cheap. At least if defined as a final product.

- Even vibe coding is difficult: you need to think very hard about what you want.

What is cheaper now are some building blocks. We just have a new definition of building blocks. But putting the blocks is still hard.

JSR_FDED 2026/04/08 00:39
设计师和产品经理向史蒂夫·乔布斯展示了这款新的 DVD 刻录应用程序的所有功能。

史蒂夫·乔布斯阻止了他们，在白板上画了一个正方形，并说“用户拖到这个正方形中的任何内容都会写入 DVD” - 这就是品味！

Designers and product managers were showing Steve Jobs all the fancy things the new app for writing DVDs could do.

Steve Jobs stopped them, drew a square on the whiteboard and said “anything the user drags into this square gets written to the DVD” - that is taste!

在 HN 中查看
boshalfoshal 2026/04/08 00:36
我认为“品味”在这一点上绝对是一个被过度使用的模因，就像科技推特在 2024 年发现了这个词并且从未停止使用它一样（与“代理”、“高杠杆”等相同）。

读完这篇文章后，我想我明白了作者的论点（*）。我认为，在工程环境中，“品味”基本上可以归结为对工程或产品方向正确或错误的固有感觉。我认为这与这里大多数人谈论的“口味”类型不同，尽管我确信产品“口味”在某种程度上与您的整体“口味”相关。工程“品味”似乎与构建系统的经验和/或对基础知识的强烈直觉更相关。我认为这与您在设计或艺术背景下可能想到的完全主观的“基于共鸣的品味”有点不同。

现在我不同意的是

1.“品味”是一条可防御的护城河

2.“味道”在某种程度上是“防人工智能”的

“品味”只有在知道该做什么并消除正确的缺陷对于加快行动速度至关重要的情况下才可以站得住脚。更快地行动和执行力才是真正的“护城河”。显然，任何认知任务，包括像“味觉”这样模糊的任务，理论上都可以由足够好的人工智能来完成。在我看来，与人工智能交流时思路清晰并不是“品味”。

具体谈论工程 - 文章讨论了产品限制和权衡。我认为这些实际上是_数据_问题，一旦解决了这些问题，权衡和解决约束就会从判断调用变成“正确”的解决方案。也就是说，如果您向人工智能提供有关业务环境的信息越多，那么实施者需要做出的判断就越少。这种想法与这里其他人已经说过的一致（真正的护城河是数据、分布、执行速度）。

我认为关于用户同理心部分有一些更有趣的事情要说，因为法学硕士在设计一些交互式界面时可能很难真正将自己置于用户展示中。但我确信这个问题也可以“解决”，或者至少可以用比现在少得多的人力来完成。

但总的来说，技术人员是最没有品味的人，所以看到这样的帖子总是很有趣。

I think "taste" is definitely an overused meme at this point, its like tech twitter discovered this word in 2024 and never stopped using it (same with "agency", "high leverage", etc).

Having read the article, I think I see the author's argument (*). I think "taste" here in an engineering context basically just comes down to an innate feeling of what engineering or product directions are right or wrong. I think this is different from the type of "taste" most people here are talking about, though I'm sure product "taste" specifically is somewhat correlated with your overall "taste." Engineering "taste" seems more correlated with experience building systems and/or strong intuitions about the fundamentals. I think this is a little different from the totally subjective, "vibes based taste" that you might think of in the context of design or art.

Now where I disagree is that

1. "taste" is a defensible moat

2. "taste" is "ai-proof" to some extent

"Taste" is only defensible to the extent that knowing what to do and cutting off the _right_ cruft is essential to moving faster. Moving faster and out executing is the real "moat" there. And obviously any cognitive task, including something as nebulous as "taste," can in theory be done by a sufficiently good AI. Clarity of thought when communicating with AI is, imo, not "taste."

Talking specifically about engineering - the article talks about product constraints and tradeoffs. I'd argue that these are actually _data_ problems, and once you solve those, tradeoffs and solving for constraints go from being a judgement call to being a "correct" solution. That is to say, if you provide more information to your AI about your business context, the less judgement _you_ as the implementer need to give. This thinking is in line with what other people here have already said (real moats are data, distribution, execution speed).

I think there's something a bit more interesting to say about the user empathy part, since it could be difficult for LLMs to truly put themselves in users shows when designing some interactive surfaces. But I'm sure that can be "solved" too, or at least, it can be done with far less human labor than it already takes.

In general though, tech people are some of the least tasteful people, so its always funny to see posts like this.

>AI and LLMs have changed one thing very quickly: competent output is now cheap.

Already wrong.

anonzzzies 2026/04/08 00:30
我使用人工智能来编写代码，我们首先检查代码并自己编写人工智能无法触及的测试。对于写作，我们几乎从不这样做，除非我们知道某些东西的请求者是无能的并且无论如何也不会阅读它；那么做任何事情都是浪费时间，但他们期望一些实质性的、好看的东西能够满足一些要求。这非常适合；一家拥有 40 层管理层的大型银行，他们都同样无能，要求建立一个“全方位的技术文档库”；其中一个人发送了一份“期望文件”，其中包含大量垃圾，表明他们甚至不知道自己在问什么，但数千页的内容是他们的期望。当然，克劳德会写道，在一小时内，notebooklm 将添加 100 个幻灯片以提高效率。乍一看，它看起来很神奇；它可能也大多准确，但谁知道呢？他们永远不会读它；没有人会。我们得到了 20m+（有很多机会发展得更大）的项目。在此之前，只有那些我们曾经输过的大型顾问（可能是那些管理层中的每个人都曾工作过）才能做到这一点。斜坡有它的目的。
I use AI for code and we review that code and write tests ourselves first which the AI cannot touch. For writing we hardly ever do, unless we know the requester of something is incompetent and will never read it anyway; then it is a waste of time to do anything, but they expect something substantial and nice looking to tick a few boxes. It is great for that; a large bank with 40 layers of management, all equally incompetent, asked for a 'all encompassing technical document vault'; one of them sent an 'expectation document' which contained so much garbage as to show they did not even know what they were asking, but 1000s of pages was the expectation. So sure, claude will write that in an hour, notebooklm will add 100 slidedecks for juiceness. At first sight it looks amazing; its probably mostly accurate as well, but who knows; they will never ever read it; no one will. We got the 20m+ (with many opportunities to grow much larger) project. Before that was only in reach of the huge consultants (where everyone in those management levels worked before probably) who we used to lose against. Slop has its purpose.

codemog 2026/04/08 00:42
这应对太疯狂了即使是 Claude 生成的简单项目也充满了错误。如果没有大量的人工干预，它根本不可能生成范围更大的项目。但是，是的，TODO 应用程序和简单的计算器都得到了有效的“解决”。和leetcode一样。我想这可能是当今许多人想象力的极限。
This cope is insane. Even simple projects generated by Claude are riddled with bugs. And there’s no way in hell it could generate a larger scoped project without a lot of manual human intervention. But yea, TODO apps and trivial calculators are effectively “solved”. Same with leetcode. I guess that’s probably the limit of many people’s imagination these days.

在 HN 中查看
ValentineC 2026/04/08 02:24
如果你把“中国”换成其他一些拥有大型海洋工程中心的国家，你的说法今天仍然适用。

中国成功地针对中文阅读市场进行复制、改进和本地化，然后通过良好地利用防火墙来驱逐竞争（尽管我想知道这是否是一个意想不到的结果）。

许多其他国家，尤其是那些没有强大的防火墙来保持本地市场购买的英语国家，仍然需要与美国科技巨头争夺“品味”。

If you replace "China" with some other countries with large offshore engineering centres, your statement would still hold true today.

China managed to copy, improve, and localise for their Chinese-reading market, then oust competition through good use of the Great Firewall (though I wonder if that specifically was an unintended consequence).

Many other countries, especially the English-speaking ones that don't have a great firewall to keep their market buying locally, still need to compete with US tech giants for "taste".

在 HN 中查看
acuozzo 2026/04/08 01:58
> 人工智能和法学硕士很快改变了一件事：合格的产出现在很便宜。

如果你正在做的事情并不是真正新颖，那当然可以。

如果您正在使用法学硕士来协助，例如数学研究的是尚未证实的问题，那么事实并非如此。

天啊，如果我们只关注软件领域：Gemini3-DeepThink、GPT-5.4pro 和 Opus 4.6 为 Hopper 和 Blackwell 编写 CUDA C++ 代码就表现得相当“meh”。

我并不是在谈论规格不完善的问题。我正在讨论使用 TMA 将带注释的 WolframLanguage 文件中的简单数学映射到 WGMMA。

> AI and LLMs have changed one thing very quickly: competent output is now cheap.

If you're working on something not truly novel, sure.

If you're using LLMs to assist in e.g. Mathematics work on as-yet-unproven problems, then this is hardly the case.

Hell, if we just stick to the software domain: Gemini3-DeepThink, GPT-5.4pro, and Opus 4.6 perform pretty "meh" writing CUDA C++ code for Hopper & Blackwell.

And I'm not talking about poorly-spec'd problems. I'm talking about mapping straightforward mathematics in annotated WolframLanguage files to WGMMA with TMA.

在 HN 中查看
liuliu 2026/04/08 02:01
我不确定你设置是否正确。您是否有可运行的 WolframLanguage 文件以便可以比较结果？您是否授予它 H100 / H200 访问权限来编译然后迭代？

我的经验是，一旦你拥有了这两个，它就会完成惊人的内核工作（Codex-5.4）。

I am not sure you set it up right. Did you have a runnable WolframLanguage file so it can compare results? Did you give it H100 / H200 access to compile and then iterate?

My experience is that once you have these two, it does amazing kernel work (Codex-5.4).

在 HN 中查看
acuozzo 2026/04/08 02:12
> 您是否有可运行的 WolframLanguage 文件以便比较结果？

是的。

> 您是否授予它 H100 / H200 访问权限来编译然后迭代？

是的，通过 Lambda.ai。另外，FWIW，我使用 --dangerously-skip-permissions 运行 claude ，并使用等效标志运行 codex 。

> 它的内核工作令人惊叹 (Codex-5.4)

特别是 WGMMA + TMA？

---

一旦 TMA 介入，Claude 和 Codex 就会无休止地旋转，直到他们抛弃 TMA 以进行较慢的回退。

我在 Claude-Code 将 Opus 4.6 推理设置为中、高和最大时观察到了这一点； “适应性思维”启用和禁用；我已经确保最大化思考标记。

除了 GPT-5.3-Codex 之外，我还在 Codex GPT-5.4 中观察到了这一点，推理工作从中等到高。

---

我也在网上观察到了这一点，正如我的 OP 中提到的，使用 GPT-5.4pro（扩展专业版）、Gemini3-DeepThink 和 Opus 4.6。

> Did you have a runnable WolframLanguage file so it can compare results?

Yes.

> Did you give it H100 / H200 access to compile and then iterate?

Yes via Lambda.ai. Also, FWIW, I run claude with --dangerously-skip-permissions and codex with the equivalent flag.

> it does amazing kernel work (Codex-5.4)

Specifically with WGMMA + TMA?

---

Once TMA gets involved both Claude and Codex spin endlessly until they dump TMA for a slower fallback.

I've observed this with Claude-Code having Opus 4.6 reasoning set to medium, high, and max; "adaptive thinking" enabled and disabled; and I've made sure to max-out thinking tokens.

I've also observed this with Codex GPT-5.4 in addition to GPT-5.3-Codex with reasoning efforts from medium to xhigh.

---

I've also observed this on the web, as mentioned in my OP, with GPT-5.4pro (Extended Pro), Gemini3-DeepThink, and Opus 4.6.

在 HN 中查看
liuliu 2026/04/08 04:27
这是信息，谢谢！是的，我观察到同样的事情，因为模型倾向于放弃（就像你说的，“放弃 TMA 以实现较慢的回退”）并且需要主动转向才能获得良好的结果。但它确实比聊天界面的一次性工作更进一步，并且比这些更了解分析/内核编码。
That is informative, thanks! Yes, I observe the same thing as the model tends to give up (like you said, "dump TMA for a slower fallback") and needs active steering to get good results. But it indeed works further than one-shot from Chat interface and knows much more about profiling / kernel coding than these.
在 HN 中查看
ux266478 2026/04/08 03:41
它不一定是像小说这样极端的东西。当面对中等复杂的语义时，模型的前沿仍然很困难。他们已经非常擅长将依赖关系粘合在一起，但是看着 Claude 在我试图给他的一个大型 xterm 项目上窒息，这真是一个相当令人失望的无意义汉堡。花了一个月的时间毫无进展，只是构建了一些东西，直到它被破坏了，代码库必须重置，他从第 1 方开始。我们已经走了很长一段路 肯定 但老实说，我们离银弹的距离和三年前一样远（对于我关心的狗屎）。我已经为明年冬天做好准备了。
It doesn't have to be anything so extreme as novel work. The frontier of models still struggle when faced with moderately complex semantics. They've gotten quite good at gluing dependencies together, but it was a rather disappointing nothingburger watching Claude choke on a large xterm project I tried to give him. Spent a month getting absolutely nowhere, just building stuff out until it was so broken the codebase had to be reset and he'd start over from square 1. We've come a long way in certain aspects, but honestly we're just as far away from the silver bullet as we were 3 years ago (for the shit I care about). I'm already bundling up for the next winter.

许多技术人员讨厌受其他人欢迎的东西。你会看到很多唠叨、抱怨和脱离现实的情况。讨厌 Instagram、“Dropbox 永远行不通”、“流行文化很糟糕”等等。

Lots of techies hate things that are popular with the rest of humanity. You see lots of nagging, complaining, and disconnected from reality takes. Hate for Instagram, "Dropbox will never work", "pop culture sucks", etc.

I'll make a mean joke: a lot of y'all better learn a trade. Plumbing, perhaps. I kid, of course, but I also wonder if it might turn out to be the eventual reality.

在 HN 中查看
ben8bit 2026/04/08 00:30
> 训练品味的实用循环

味道便宜。品味（或者至少是它的基本版本）是你在职业生涯开始时就开始的事情。味道会告诉你“这太酷了”，或者“我不知道为什么，但这看起来不错”。法学硕士不会复制这一点，因为它不是人类，而且味道也不是你能做出来的。现在 - 制作“看起来正确”的东西很难，而且因为法学硕士正在大量生产中间 - 中间正在转移到其他地方。就像有钱人在夏天一样。

> A practical loop for training taste

Taste is cheap. Taste (or a rudimentary version of it at least) is something you start with at the beginning of your career. Taste is the thing that tells you "this is fucking cool", or "I don't know why but this just looks right". LLM's are not going to replicate that because it's not a human and taste isn't something you can make. Now - MAKING something that "looks right" is hard, and because LLM's are churning out the middle - the middle is moving somewhere else. Just like rich people during the summer.

> 随着时间的推移，这会改变您的工作方式。你不再为了抛光本身而欣赏它。你会更快地发现空洞的具体内容、借用的语气和虚假的自信。

I agree.

> Over time, this changes how you work. You stop admiring polish for its own sake. You get faster at spotting empty specificity, borrowed tone, and fake confidence.

“Empty specificity, borrowed tone and fake confidence” describe the article itself.

_dwt 2026/04/08 00:38
这里的情况越来越糟糕；上周，我看到至少三篇明显由人工智能撰写的“反人工智能”或“批评人工智能”的文章登上了头版。我情不自禁地想起了比尔·希克斯（Bill Hicks）对营销的看法：“这里每个从事营销工作的人现在都在想同样的事情：‘哦，太酷了。比尔正在争取反营销的钱。那是一个巨大的市场。’”

I can't help but think about Bill Hicks on marketing: "Everyone here who’s in marketing is now thinkin' the same thing: 'Oh, cool. Bill's going for that anti-marketing dollar. That's a huge market.'"

在 HN 中查看
scared_together 2026/04/08 01:13
这也可能是“训练品味的实用循环”部分中描述的循环的第一次迭代。这与其说是“恶作剧”，不如说是“利用 HN 观众来喂机器”。

循环（为简洁起见，剪掉了一些点）：

> 1. 从您的一周中挑选一件高杠杆工件。一段……

> 2.用AI模型生成10到20个版本。

> 3. 对于每个版本，写一个以“失败是因为……”开头的句子

> 4. 重写最强版本，加硬约束……

> 5. 将最终版本发送到真实的地方并观察会发生什么。

It’s also possible this is the first iteration of the loop described in the “A practical loop for training taste” section. Which would be less of a “prank” and more of “using the HN audience to feed the machine”.

The loop (some points snipped for brevity):

> 1. Pick one high-leverage artifact from your week. A paragraph…

> 2. Generate 10 to 20 versions with an AI model.

> 3. For each version, write one sentence that starts with "fails because..."

> 4. Rewrite the strongest version with a hard constraint…

> 5. Ship the final version somewhere real and observe what happens.

echelon 2026/04/08 00:13
不——从表面上看，我们的工作价值已经下降。我们职业生涯的整个供需经济学正在眨眼间发生变化。

有人试图弄清楚这意味着什么以及在哪里创造价值。 “品味是唯一的护城河”就是这样的假设之一。 “高级工程师就没问题”是另一个说法。

现在一切都充满泡沫，我们即将迎来疯狂的 2026 年。

No - at face value, our work has diminished value. The entire supply and demand economics of our careers is changing in the blink of an eye.

There are people trying to figure out what this means and where to create value. "Taste is the only moat" is one such hypothesis. "Senior engineers will be fine" is another.

Everything is super frothy right now and we're in for a wild 2026.

heliumtera 2026/04/08 01:30
